India's energy sector is facing a crucial turning point.

While most petrochemical and refining stocks corrected recently, petrol prices remain elevated across major cities. The next few quarters could decide whether the sector enters a fresh upcycle or faces further pressure.

📊 LATEST STOCK PERFORMANCE

🔻 Reliance: -1.4%
🔻 IOC: -1.83%
🔻 BPCL: -3.07%
🔻 HPCL: -2.37%
🔻 MRPL: -3.07%
🔻 Oil India: -0.58%

🟢 ONGC: +0.39%
🟢 Petronet LNG: +0.16%
🟢 Vedanta Oil: +4.95%

⛽PETROL PRICES STILL HIGH

• Mumbai – ₹111.21/litre
• Bangalore – ₹110.93/litre
• Chennai – ₹107.74/litre
• Bhubaneswar – ₹108.97/litre
• Guntur – ₹117.68/litre

Higher fuel prices indicate that crude oil costs remain elevated, impacting both consumers and refiners.

🔍 WHAT IS PRESSURING THE SECTOR?

❌ Rising Global Crude Oil Prices

Higher crude increases raw material costs for refiners and petrochemical companies.

❌ Weak Refining Margins (GRMs)

Lower refining spreads directly impact profits of IOC, BPCL, HPCL and MRPL.

❌ Global Economic Slowdown

Slower industrial activity reduces demand for fuels and petrochemical products.

❌ China Demand Uncertainty

China remains one of the world's largest energy consumers. Weak demand impacts global pricing.

❌ Rupee Depreciation

A weaker rupee makes crude imports more expensive.

🚀 WHAT CAN TRIGGER A RECOVERY?

✅ Brent Crude Stabilizes Below $75-80

Lower input costs improve profitability of OMCs.

✅ Improvement In GRMs

Even a small improvement in refining margins can significantly boost earnings.

✅ Strong Domestic Fuel Demand

India remains one of the fastest-growing energy markets globally.

✅ China Stimulus Measures

A recovery in manufacturing activity can boost petrochemical demand.

✅ Natural Gas Consumption Growth

Petronet LNG and gas infrastructure companies benefit from India's energy transition.

🏛️ WHAT CORRECTIVE DECISIONS CAN THE GOVERNMENT TAKE?

1️⃣ Reduce Excise Duty on Fuel

Can support demand and ease inflationary pressures.

2️⃣ Allow Timely Fuel Price Revisions

Helps OMCs maintain healthy marketing margins.

3️⃣ Increase Strategic Crude Reserves

Protects India from global supply shocks and geopolitical disruptions.

4️⃣ Accelerate Natural Gas Adoption

Higher gas penetration reduces dependence on imported crude oil.

5️⃣ Boost Domestic Oil & Gas Exploration

Benefits ONGC, Oil India and improves energy security.

6️⃣ Support Petrochemical Manufacturing

PLI schemes and infrastructure investments can improve sector competitiveness.

7️⃣ Reduce Windfall Taxes

Can improve profitability and investment sentiment for upstream producers.

📈 BIGGEST BENEFICIARIES IF CONDITIONS IMPROVE

🔥 ONGC
Higher crude prices directly boost realizations.

🔥 Oil India
Strong leverage to exploration and production growth.

🔥 Reliance Industries
Benefits from recovery in petrochemicals, refining margins and strong consumer businesses.

🔥 BPCL / IOC / HPCL
Potential earnings rebound if GRMs improve and fuel pricing remains favorable.

🔥 Petronet LNG
Long-term beneficiary of India's clean energy transition.

💡 INVESTOR TAKEAWAY

The current weakness appears cyclical rather than structural.

India's energy demand is expected to keep growing over the next decade driven by:
✅ Rising vehicle ownership
✅ Industrial expansion
✅ Infrastructure spending
✅ Aviation growth
✅ Increasing petrochemical consumption

The key variables to monitor are:

📌 Brent Crude Prices
📌 Refining Margins (GRMs)
📌 Government Fuel Policies
📌 China Demand Recovery
📌 Rupee-Dollar Movement
📌 LNG Demand Growth

The next 6-12 months could create significant opportunities in quality energy stocks if crude stabilizes and margins recover.

#Forex #GBPUSD UPDATE – Week Ahead Special
Commencing Monday 22 June 2026

Live Spot GBP/USD: 1.3233

As this is the weekend prior to the week commencing 22 June, this reflects the Friday 19 June close.

1. Macro and Political Drivers (Comprehensive Week-Ahead Assessment)

The week commencing 22 June 2026 follows the heavy central bank and inflation focus of mid-June and is expected to be lighter in terms of high-impact events, but still features key US flash PMI data and housing/leading indicators that will influence GBPUSD. Geopolitics remains a supportive factor for the dollar. The fragile US-Iran ceasefire has reduced immediate safe-haven flows, but energy prices continue to underpin inflation concerns and the Federal Reserve’s restrictive stance.

Spot WTI Crude is currently around 76.55 and Brent Crude around 80.40. This energy environment keeps global inflation expectations elevated, reinforcing higher-for-longer US rates.

In the United States, the Federal Reserve remains data-dependent and hawkish following the June FOMC meeting. Rate-cut expectations are largely deferred into late 2026. Any resilient data, particularly the flash PMIs and PCE later in the week, will strengthen the dollar via yield support and policy divergence.

In the United Kingdom, sterling continues to carry a pronounced political risk premium. Ongoing questions around fiscal credibility, leadership stability, and domestic policy uncertainty leave GBP vulnerable to negative headlines. Combined with softer underlying growth dynamics, this creates clear asymmetric downside risk for cable. UK flash PMI data on Tuesday will be closely watched for any signs of weakness that could weigh further on sterling.

Full Economic Calendar – Key Events for GBPUSD (22–26 June 2026)

Monday 22 June

Limited high-impact releases scheduled for both US and UK. Focus on any overnight developments, minor data flows, or central bank speeches.

Tuesday 23 June (Key data day)

US S&P Global Flash Manufacturing & Services PMI (June) – Medium-to-high impact. Provides early insight into business activity. Stronger-than-expected prints would support the dollar.
UK S&P Global Flash Manufacturing, Services & Composite PMI (June) – Medium-to-high impact (11:30 BST). Key gauge of UK private sector activity. Weaker-than-expected prints would highlight softening growth and add downside pressure on GBP.
US ADP Employment Change (weekly) – Medium impact.

Wednesday 24 June

US New Home Sales (May) – Medium impact.
US Leading Economic Indicators (May) – Medium impact. These provide a forward-looking view of the US economy. Stronger data would reinforce USD strength.

Thursday 25 June

US PCE Price Index (May) – High impact. Core PCE is the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Hotter-than-expected prints would strongly support the dollar.
US GDP Final (Q1) – Medium-to-high impact.
US Initial Jobless Claims – Medium impact.
US Durable Goods Orders (May) – Medium impact.

Friday 26 June

US Wholesale Inventories (May) – Medium impact.
University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Final (June) – Medium impact.
Limited UK high-impact data scheduled.

Overall, this is a relatively quiet week compared to mid-June, with the main catalysts being the US and UK flash PMIs on Tuesday and US PCE/GDP on Thursday. Stronger US data would reinforce selling pressure on GBP. Softer prints could allow temporary relief rallies in cable.

2. DXY and Yield Correlation (Real-Time Context)

The DXY remains resilient near recent highs. US two-year yields continue to hold a clear premium over UK equivalents, creating a persistent headwind for any sterling recovery attempts. This correlation is expected to remain firm this week. Sterling strength, where seen, is likely to be sold into as long as the yield spread favours the dollar.

3. Options and Gamma Structure

At current levels near 1.3233, GBPUSD sits near a key gamma pivot zone. Significant upside gamma concentration lies around 1.3250–1.3280, while a strong downside magnet exists near 1.3180–1.3150. Hedging flows are expected to keep price action relatively range-bound until Tuesday’s flash PMI data provides a clearer catalyst. A decisive break above 1.3280 could trigger accelerated short-covering, while a break below 1.3180 would open lower liquidity pools.

4. Liquidity and Order Flow Map

Buy-side liquidity clusters are visible above 1.3250–1.3280 (short stops). Major sell-side pools are concentrated below at 1.3180 and 1.3150. Institutional order flow continues to demonstrate a clear preference for selling sterling strength. The highest-probability edge remains fading rallies into resistance rather than chasing dips in the absence of a strong catalyst.

5. X (Twitter) – Macro Flow from Last 24 Hours Only

Recent posts on X in the last 24 hours show limited high-quality GBPUSD-specific trading analysis, with focus shifting toward the upcoming US and UK flash PMIs. Sentiment appears quiet and cautiously bearish to neutral. Traders are highlighting distribution patterns on higher timeframes and warning against chasing short-term bounces ahead of the data. Overall tone remains net negative for cable, with emphasis on selling any strength into key resistance zones.

6. Technical Structure (Detailed Analysis)

GBPUSD continues to exhibit classic distribution behaviour after repeated failed attempts to sustain upside momentum. The pair is trading within a well-defined range with clear higher-timeframe resistance.

Key Levels for the Week:

Resistance: 1.3250–1.3280 (primary sell zone), then 1.3320–1.3350

Support: 1.3180 (major pivot), 1.3150, and 1.3100

Technical indicators on daily and 4H timeframes remain neutral to mildly bearish, supporting a strategy of selling strength rather than chasing weakness.

7. Week-Ahead Scenario Model & Bias

Primary Scenario (65% probability): Range trading with repeated selling into rallies. Stronger-than-expected US flash PMIs on Tuesday and resilient PCE/GDP data on Thursday would drive cable toward 1.3150–1.3100 by week close.

Secondary Scenario (35% probability): Softer-than-expected US and UK data triggers a short-covering squeeze toward 1.3320–1.3350, though any strength is expected to be faded aggressively given the broader macro and political backdrop.

Definitive Week-Ahead Trade Suggestion

Bias: Preferentially bearish – sell rallies.

Trade: Sell GBP/USD on rallies toward 1.3250–1.3280 or at current levels on strength. Stop Loss: 1.3320 (risk ~85–95 pips depending on exact entry). Take Profit: Initial target 1.3150, extension to 1.3100–1.3080 on strong follow-through (potential reward 130–180+ pips).

This setup maintains a minimum 1:2 risk-reward ratio with prudent position sizing and aligns fully with the macro drivers, technical distribution, gamma structure, and liquidity profile.

Invalidation: A sustained daily close above 1.3320 would shift the short-term view to neutral-to-bullish and require full reassessment.

Conclusion

The week ahead commencing 22 June 2026 is expected to be relatively quiet in terms of high-impact events compared to mid-June, with the main focus on US and UK flash PMIs on Tuesday and US PCE/GDP data on Thursday. The structural backdrop continues to favour the dollar. Sterling faces persistent political risk and growth headwinds, making any rallies attractive selling opportunities. Discipline, strict risk management, and a consistent bias toward selling strength remain the optimal approach. Expect volatility to increase around Tuesday’s flash PMI releases.

I am a professional former interbank currency trader now trading privately, and any views expressed represent my own trading opinions and personal trading activity and are not investment advice or suitable for all market participants.

Iran can’t close the Strait of Hormuz because they have no navy, air force, air defense, or working command and control, as America obliterated them.

Either all ships are escorted by the US Navy 24/7, or oil tankers run at night like they’re doing now.

Either way, it’s not closed.

Strait of Hormuz news: Iran announces blockade of shipping lanes (pending further confirmation)

If true, this is a typical escalation of geopolitical risk, and the market will immediately react to it in oil prices.

The impact on US stocks can be roughly divided into three lines:

Stronger Energy: Expectations of rising oil prices will directly benefit upstream companies such as $XLE/ $XOP,  $CVX, $XOM, and $COP.

Inflation Returns to Trading: If oil prices continue to rise, inflation expectations will resurface, and expectations of interest rate cuts may be further postponed. This will put pressure on growth stocks (especially high-valuation technology/semiconductor stocks).

Pressure on Risk Assets + Increased Volatility: Technology stocks may weaken in the short term, while the VIX will rise, leading to a greater preference for defensive assets.

However, it's important to note that the initial reaction to such news is often "emotional pricing." The real key is whether the news is actually implemented and whether there will be any military/diplomatic escalation.

Short-term trading perspective:

Energy: Slightly stronger; Technology: Slightly under pressure; Market: Increased volatility

This type of market is not suitable for chasing; it's more about timing and hedging. If the upgrade is confirmed later, this won't be a one-day market trend.#Geopolitics #OilPrices #USStocks #EnergySector

The toll question reveals the underlying leverage structure. If the US imposes one, it claims formal control over an international waterway — legally complex, especially with China, India, and Japan watching. If Iran imposes one unilaterally, it's a sanctions violation and a casus belli. Neither happens: the toll was always rhetorical positioning. What matters commercially is the insurance premium, which is already a de facto toll that markets pay regardless of official announcements. Brent at $80 with US crude inventories at 1985 lows is not a fearful price; it's a market that hasn't fully priced the physical picture yet.The toll question reveals the underlying leverage structure. "The good news: The Strait of Hormuz is open again, after Iran and the United States signed a memorandum of understanding this week.

The bad news: It may be too late.

Oil hasn’t been coming out of the Middle East for nearly four months. All told, the world lost 1.15 billion barrels of oil supply during the war, according to analytics firm Kpler.
That has left the oil market in a precarious state, and it’s rapidly approaching a breaking point. 

The International Energy Administration’s strategic petroleum reserves are at their lowest levels since 1990. The American emergency reserve is at a 43-year low. And commercial inventories have hit operational stress levels.

“You want to see bedlam?” President Donald Trump said at the G7 in Versailles Wednesday. “We run out of reserves in about four weeks.”

Trump is right. But reopening the strait this week may not get oil out of the Persian Gulf fast enough to prevent crude inventories from effectively running to empty.

Oil prices may have to go higher again."

CNN

Week-Ahead Brief | June 22-26 2026 | SPX 7500.58 close (Thursday OPEX)

The cheapest options of the year going into the biggest macro week. Five days mapped. Daily levels and scenarios.

Goldman warned Friday that this week looks calm but isn't. Here is why.

Three signals are all pricing the same thing. Monday options trade at 8.91 percent vol, the cheapest pricing since Christmas holiday week. COR1M sits at 8.02. Below 8 is extreme. When correlation snaps back, the move is usually violent. June OPEX cleared Thursday with more call positioning than puts, leaving dealer hedges to unwind Monday and Tuesday. Three asymmetric signals stacked into a week with Core PCE Thursday.

The week builds straight into PCE. Monday Fed Waller speech 9 AM kicks off post-FOMC commentary. Tuesday PMI Flash gives the first economic activity read. Wednesday brings Stress Test results plus Micron earnings after-hours. Thursday Core PCE is the headline event. Friday closes Q2 with pension rebalancing flow last 30 minutes.

Iran closed Hormuz again Saturday as talks open in Switzerland Sunday with Lebanon attacks still live. Watch Brent at the Monday open for the inflation read.

Thursday closed at 7500.58 and took back the pivot. 7500 anchors the week. 7440 to 7480 EMA confluence holds the floor. Above 7600 the post-OPEX residual call wall caps the upside. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

THE WEEK AT A GLANCE

Mon 6/22 (CAUTION flag): Fed Waller speech 9 AM. Post-OPEX dealer unwind window starts. Light data day. Bill auctions only.

Tue 6/23 (SQUEEZE flag): ADP weekly 8:15 AM. S&P Global PMI Flash 9:45 AM (first activity read). Richmond Fed 10 AM. 2-Year Note Auction 1 PM. CCL pre-market and FDX after-hours earnings.

Wed 6/24 (CALM flag): Building Permits and New Home Sales 10 AM. EIA Crude 10:30 AM. 5-Year Note Auction 1 PM. Fed Bank Stress Test Results 4 PM. Micron earnings after-hours (11.98 percent implied move). TCOM after-hours.

Thu 6/25 (SQUEEZE flag): Core PCE Price Index 8:30 AM. Durable Goods 8:30 AM. GDP Q1 Final 8:30 AM. Jobless Claims 8:30 AM. Fed Williams 3:40 PM. Fed Goolsbee 6:30 PM. The headline event of the week.

Fri 6/26 (CALM flag): Goods Trade Balance 8:30 AM. Michigan Sentiment Final 10 AM. Baker Hughes Rig Count 1 PM. Last full trading day of Q2. Quarter End rebalancing flow into the close.

THE VOL PICTURE

Vol is priced for a quiet week into PCE Thursday. That pricing is the asymmetric part of the setup.

Monday at-the-money options trade at 8.91 percent IV. Goldman flagged that level approaches the 7 percent lower bound seen during the Christmas holiday week. That is holiday-week pricing for a week that has Fed Waller speaking Monday morning, PMI Flash Tuesday, Stress Test results Wednesday, Core PCE Thursday, and quarter-end Friday. The options market is paying almost nothing for protection on Monday and Tuesday.

The straddles tell the same story. Monday options price an 85 point expected move from the close. Friday options price 110 points. The 25 point gap is the entire week of risk premium. Most of it lives in Thursday's PCE print and Friday's month-end flow.

Goldman also flagged the structural imbalance from Thursday's OPEX. The June expiry cycle had more call delta notional than puts. When those positions roll off, dealer hedges associated with them unwind, and that can contribute to elevated volatility. The institutional research desks are openly warning about Monday and Tuesday being trap days.

VVIX sits at 88.43, right at its historical mean of 85. After running to 94.53 Wednesday on the hawkish dot plot, vol-of-vol normalized into Thursday's OPEX rally. Going into a PCE week with the dot plot now showing rate hikes as a real possibility, VVIX at the historical baseline is not paying up for protection. That mirrors what the index options market is doing.

The vol curve says the market thinks Monday and Tuesday are sleepy then vol builds into PCE Thursday and quarter-end Friday. If post-OPEX dealer unwind hits as Goldman called out, that assumption breaks fast and Monday or Tuesday becomes the surprise vol expansion.

WHAT HAPPENED THURSDAY OPEX

SPX closed 7500.58, up 80.48 points. Opened 7487.36. Session low 7468.32. Session high 7511.07. Reclaimed the 7500 pivot that broke Wednesday post-FOMC. Closed RIGHT AT the level. Range only 42.75 points which is tight for an OPEX day. Vol absorbed cleanly.

Dow +0.14 percent. Russell 2000 +2.12 percent (smallcaps led). Nasdaq +1.91 percent on Intel-Apple chip news. Brent oil dropped on Iran deal optimism then bounced back to $80 Friday on Hormuz uncertainty.

AAII Sentiment for week ending 6/17 showed Bullish 36.6 percent, Bearish 39.4 percent. More bearish than bullish, more bearish than the historical average of 31 percent. That is a contrarian-bullish positioning setup, especially with smallcaps showing the strongest sentiment in the special question at 27.4 percent.

The Sep 15 Fed meeting now prices 50 percent No Change and 38 percent 25 bps Hike. Before Wednesday the hike probability was near zero. The hawkish dot plot repriced the curve. PCE Thursday is the next test.

THE WEEK'S BIG STORY

Four threads pull on this week.

1. Post-OPEX dealer unwind Monday and Tuesday. Goldman flagged June OPEX had more call delta notional than puts. When call hedges unwind without put hedges balancing them, the unwind tilts the tape. Vol can expand from compressed levels fast.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Core PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. After a hawkish dot plot now showing 38 percent hike probability at September, any upside surprise puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

3. Iran 60-day clock and Hormuz. Iran declared Hormuz closed Saturday, calling it the IRGC's first operational step. US-Iran talks open Sunday in Switzerland. Markets have so far dismissed the closure as rhetoric since 55 ships transited Hormuz Saturday despite the announcement. But Brent at $80 and Israel still attacking Lebanon mean energy can spike Monday open if Sunday talks fail. The peace deal is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday. Pension rebalancing flow possible last 30 minutes of the session. Q2 ends Friday June 26 since June 30 is a Tuesday. Watch closing tape.

THE WEEK'S RISKS

1. Goldman's post-OPEX dealer unwind warning Monday and Tuesday. Vol priced for calm. The map shows 7660 and 7650 call walls from OPEX residual on Monday that vanish Tuesday. The transition between days is where vol can fire.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Hawkish dot plot showed 38 percent hike probability at September meeting. Hot PCE puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Cool PCE relieves the entire curve.

3. Iran Hormuz closure and Lebanon. Talks open Switzerland Sunday. If they fail or stall, Brent pops Monday open and feeds the inflation story into PCE Thursday. 55 ships still transited Hormuz Saturday so the immediate impact is rhetoric not reality. But the situation is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday pension rebalancing. If Q2 ends positive, expect equity selling and bond buying last 30 minutes. Could be a setup for a Monday June 29 reversal if positioning unwinds.

RULES

1. Vol is priced at holiday-week levels. The setup is asymmetric. Trade smaller until the post-OPEX unwind window clears.

2. 7500 is the anchor for the entire week. Hold it Monday and bulls have a path. Lose it and the structure changes.

3. 7440 to 7480 is the moving average confluence zone. 20D EMA 7441.01 + 20D SMA 7481.88. The strongest technical support cluster of the week sits here.

4. Goldman warned about post-OPEX dealer unwind hitting Monday and Tuesday. Vol can expand fast from compressed levels. Treat all levels as breakable Monday and Tuesday.

5. The 7345 acceleration zone on Monday's put map is the worst-case break. Massive sellers stacked. If 7350 fails, the move accelerates.

6. PCE Thursday is the headline event. Position smaller into the print. The 8:30 AM data drop includes PCE + Durable Goods + GDP + Jobless Claims all at once. Most catalyst-heavy moment of the year so far.

7. The 7320 and 7310 paired levels on Thursday's book look like an insurance trade. Watch how they develop into PCE.

8. Friday is quarter-end. Last 30 minutes can be one-directional based on Q2 performance. Stay flat or hedge into the close.

9. Iran headlines from Sunday's Switzerland talks set the Monday open tone. If Brent pops, the inflation story for PCE Thursday gets louder.

10. Above 7500 we stay bullish into PCE. Below 7400 we stay bearish into PCE. The middle is the chop zone where most of the week lives.

Not financial advice. Verify all levels before trading.

It is by now an open secret in Washington DC that the vicious contempt for American public opinion which pervades the current regime is made possible by a corrupt intent to frustrate by various machinations the choices made by the American people in the upcoming November general election. Democratic majority leader Schumer earlier this year had announced the formation of a task force to address voter suppression and election protection during the rest of 2026. This task force has just conducted what appears to be its first publicized meeting. In addition to Schumer, the meeting was attended by Warner of Virginia, Durbin of Illinois, Schiff of California, Warnock of Georgia, and Padilla of California, all members of the working group.  Also in attendance was a group of experts, including former Attorney General Eric Holder, leading election attorneys Marc Elias of Democracy Docket and Norm Eisen of Brookings, CNN, and the States United the Democracy Center, plus representatives of Democracy Forward and the Nonpartisan Protect Democracy. The meeting took the form of a tabletop drill designed to game out a number of possible election scenarios and what to do about them. Senator Warnock was asked about this activity on one of last Sunday’s political talk shows. The most detailed account of the proceedings appears in Politico of June 11, 2026, which identifies three of the scenarios that were examined. These were as follows:

‘One of the scenarios hypothesized a foreign influence operation involving AI-generated deepfakes and a federal crackdown on media attempting to report on the disinformation campaign.

‘Another tracked how claims of systemic election fraud could prompt armed citizens to patrol voting sites and ballot drop boxes — and lead Trump to send federal agents to polling locations to “protect the election.”

‘A third played out what would happen if the Department of Justice disrupts certifications in critical races by seizing ballots to investigate “election administration irregularities.”’ (Politico, June 11, 2026)

It would also be important to identify government officials (if any) in Congress, the executive branch and its independent agencies, the federal and state judiciaries, and the military down to the brigade or battalion level who might be sympathetic to efforts favoring the overthrow of the United States government, which we have recently seen and are determined not to experience again.

A NEW STAB IN THE BACK LEGEND FOR USE BY US FASCISTS?

There is yet another way in which the stunning capitulation of the current US regime to Iranian blackmail might be used in the not so distant future to undermine all political parties, and thus the democratic system, at least in the form in which it exists today. The basis for this danger is that no US political party has focused on defending the US Armed Forces during this conflict. The Republican party line was the usual glorification of Trump, while the Democrats were the spirit of negation, demanding an abrupt and humiliating cessation of hostilities followed by cut and run. Neither focused on the way Trump prevented the US military from winning a victory which was or is still within reach. This is the danger of a new “stab in the back legend” on the model of a demagogic theme that was very common among German fascists in the years after their nation’s defeat in World War I. This infamous Dolchstoßlegende or stab in the back legend, which was the principal basis of Hitler’s demagogy in the first few years of his fascist agitation.

The Dolchstoßlegende was based on the argument that the German army on the Western front of World War I had never been militarily defeated, but had rather been betrayed – betrayed by political forces in Berlin, with inevitable emphasis on Social Democrats, Bolsheviks, Jews, liberals, corrupt bureaucrats, and the like. This was a way of blaming the German defeat on the German revolution of November 1918, which overthrew the German Empire, forced the Emperor William II to flee the country for exile in the Netherlands, and witnessed the proclamation of a Republic on November 9, 1918 by the Social Democrat Schliemann and the proclamation of a Socialist Republic by Karl Liebknecht on that same day. Some specious credibility was given to this legend by the fact that Germany had requested an armistice at a time when its army was still fighting in France and Belgium and had not been driven back onto German territory.  But the basic contention must be regarded as a deliberate lie, as convincing evidence indicates.

What evidence? Some very authoritative evidence came from none other than the German Supreme Commander, Field Marshal Paul von Hindenburg, an east Elbian Junker nobleman who later served as Germany's Reichspraesident. Hindenburg was interviewed in 1918, just after the November 11 armistice, by the American journalist George Seldes. In the course of an exclusive interview with von Hindenburg, Seldes respectfully asked him, “Who won the war?” Much of the press, even in the United States, had been giving the credit to France, Britain, Italy, and/or Belgium. But von Hindenburg’s answer was blunt:

“The American infantry in the Argonne won the war.”

This refers to the autumn 1918 battle known in America as the Meuse-Argonne in the easternn French province of Lorraie (today's Grand Est), which is still considered the largest and deadliest battle in which American forces have ever participated. Hindenburg explained that the German casualties inflicted by the US forces in the Meuse-Argonne had consumed so many of his already depleted divisions that it had become impossible for him to maintain a coherent line of defense, so that from a purely military point of view, there was nothing else to do but ask for a cease-fire or armistice.  The US advantage was the availability of an ample  supply of fresh troops and replacements to throw into battle, which the Germans at this point were too exhausted to match. In other words, von Hindenburg explicitly conceded that the German army had indeed been militarily defeated in the field, beyond all doubt or quibble.  This meant that the stab in the back legend was a lie, nothing but a piece of fake propaganda invented for demagogic purposes by Hitler, Goebbels, and their ilk.

Up until the Meuse-Argonne, von Hindenburg explained, the Germans had been hoping to engineer a stalemate or frozen conflict along a front line still located in France and Belgium, leading before long to a negotiated peace.

As an official US war correspondent, Seldes had been inducted, according to the established procedures of that time, into the US Army as an officer. For various reasons his interview with Hindenburg excited the anger of the authoritarian US commanding General Pershing and his clique at US Expeditionary Force Headquarters in Chaumont, France. Pershing put Seldes on trial in a court-martial for "breaking the armistice," of which he was convicted. Part of the verdict was that Seldes was forbidden to ever publish or divulge the content of his interview with Hindenburg. This meant that the world at large was denied this important knowledge about the main grievance cited by the Nazis for more than a decade, since Hindenburg never repeated this frank analysis and soon began preaching the stab in the back lie himself for reasons of rank political opportunism. Seldes eventually published the story in his book You Can’t Print That! (New York: Payson and Clarke, 1929).
